items.subject: neuroscience
items.topic: neuroplasticity and the brain's ability to rewire itself
items.transcript: Neuroplasticity is the brain's ability to reorganize itself by forming new connections between its cells throughout life. For a long time, scientists assumed the adult brain was essentially fixed. Once you reached maturity, the thinking went, your wiring was set and could not change. We now know that view is wrong. The brain is constantly remodeling itself in response to what we do and experience. Let me define the key idea more precisely. Neurons communicate across tiny gaps called synapses. When two neurons fire together repeatedly, the connection between them strengthens; when a pathway goes unused, it gradually weakens. In other words, cells that fire together tend to wire together. Here's a striking example. Researchers studied London taxi drivers, who must memorize the city's thousands of streets. Brain scans revealed that a region called the hippocampus, which is involved in spatial memory, was noticeably larger in these drivers than in other people. And the longer someone had driven a taxi, the larger that region tended to be. Their brains had physically adapted to the demands of the job. This has real consequences. After a stroke damages part of the brain, patients can sometimes recover lost abilities because healthy regions take over the functions of the damaged area. Therapy that repeatedly exercises a weakened skill encourages the brain to rebuild those pathways. So the brain isn't a fixed machine you're stuck with. It's more like a living landscape, reshaped by everything you practice and repeat.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The brain's ability to reorganize its connections throughout life
options.B: How London taxi drivers memorize thousands of city streets
options.C: How a stroke damages specific regions of the human brain
options.D: How neurons send signals across the gaps between them

questions.explanation: A 正确：全篇主题是神经可塑性，即大脑终生都能重组连接。B 太狭窄：出租车司机只是支撑主题的一个例子。C 话题错位：中风只是最后用来说明可塑性的应用，不是主题。D 表面词陷阱：借用了课上“突触/信号”等词，但传递信号并非讲座的核心主题。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what happens to a neural pathway that goes unused?

options.A: It is quickly replaced by a brand-new neuron
options.B: The connection between the neurons gradually grows weaker
options.C: It sends its signals to the hippocampus for storage
options.D: The connection between the neurons becomes even stronger

questions.explanation: B 正确：教授说未被使用的通路会逐渐减弱。A 错误细节：课上讲的是连接强弱变化，未提到用新神经元替换。C 话题相邻：海马体虽被提到，但与“未用通路”无关。D 逻辑反转：与“减弱”相反，强化是发生在反复共同放电时。

questions.stem: Why does the professor discuss London taxi drivers?

options.A: To explain how people memorize long lists of directions
options.B: To argue that driving is the best way to grow the brain
options.C: To give concrete evidence that experience reshapes the brain
options.D: To describe how taxi drivers learn the city's street layout

questions.explanation: C 正确：司机的例子是为了用脑扫描证据说明经验会实实在在改变大脑结构。A 目的错位：重点不是记路方法本身。B 过度推断：教授没有主张开车是最佳的增脑方式。D 字面复述：只是复述了例子讲了什么，而非解释为何要举这个例子。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about recovery after a stroke?

options.A: All stroke patients fully regain every ability they lost
options.B: Damaged brain regions repair themselves without any therapy
options.C: Strokes mainly affect the brain's spatial memory center
options.D: Undamaged regions can take on tasks once handled elsewhere

questions.explanation: D 正确：教授暗示健康脑区能接管受损区域的功能，这正是可塑性带来的恢复。A 过度概括：说“所有病人完全恢复一切能力”太绝对。B 逻辑反转：教授强调需要反复练习的治疗，而非受损区自行修复。C 话题相邻：把海马体的例子错误泛化为中风主要影响空间记忆。

items.subject: history
items.topic: how ice harvesting shaped nineteenth-century commerce
items.transcript: Before mechanical refrigeration existed, how did people keep food cold in the summer? For much of the nineteenth century, the answer came from a surprising industry: natural ice harvesting. Ice cut from frozen lakes and rivers became a major commercial product, and it reshaped how people ate and traded. The business was pioneered by a New England merchant named Frederic Tudor, sometimes called the Ice King. In the early 1800s, Tudor had an unusual idea: cut ice from ponds in Massachusetts during winter and ship it to warm places that had never seen it. His first shipments to the Caribbean largely melted, and he lost money for years. The breakthrough came from better insulation. Workers learned that packing the ice in sawdust, a cheap waste product from lumber mills, dramatically slowed melting. With sawdust insulation, ice could survive long ocean voyages. Once the technique was proven, the trade exploded. Crews used horse-drawn cutters to score frozen lakes into a grid, then sawed out uniform blocks and stored them in insulated icehouses. Ice from New England was shipped as far as India, thousands of miles away, and much of it arrived intact. Closer to home, a network of icehouses and delivery wagons brought ice to city households and businesses. This cold chain changed daily life. Breweries could brew year-round, fresh fish and meat could travel farther, and ordinary families could store perishable food. So an industry built on frozen water helped lay the groundwork for the modern food economy, well before the electric refrigerator arrived.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How Frederic Tudor lost money on his first shipments
options.B: How the natural ice trade reshaped nineteenth-century commerce
options.C: How the electric refrigerator replaced older cooling methods
options.D: How sawdust was produced in New England lumber mills

questions.explanation: B 正确：讲座主题是天然采冰业如何重塑十九世纪的商业与生活。A 太狭窄：初期亏损只是故事的一个阶段。C 话题错位：电冰箱只在结尾一句作为对照，不是内容。D 表面词陷阱：锯末只是保温材料，其生产过程并非主题。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what made long-distance ice shipping practical?

options.A: Cutting the ice into much smaller, lighter blocks
options.B: Shipping only during the coldest winter months
options.C: Packing the ice in sawdust to slow the melting
options.D: Exposing the ice to open air so it stayed frozen

questions.explanation: C 正确：教授说用锯末包裹冰块可大幅减缓融化，使长途运输成为可能。A 错误细节：讲的是切成大小一致的方块，未提为运输而切小。B 话题相邻：季节听似合理，但课上归因于保温而非发运时间。D 逻辑反转：暴露在空气中只会加速融化，与保温相反。

questions.stem: According to the professor, how far did New England ice travel?

options.A: Only to nearby towns within New England itself
options.B: Mostly to breweries along the Atlantic coast
options.C: Nowhere, because it always melted before arriving
options.D: As far as India, thousands of miles away

questions.explanation: D 正确：教授明确说新英格兰的冰远运至印度、行程数千英里，且多能完好抵达。A 错误细节：与“远达印度”矛盾。B 话题相邻：酿酒厂被提到，但那是本地用途，不是最远距离。C 逻辑反转：技术成熟后大多完好送达，并非全部融化。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the ice trade's significance?

options.A: It helped prepare the way for the modern food economy
options.B: It completely eliminated all food spoilage everywhere
options.C: It made fresh food harder for families to obtain
options.D: It was only useful for brewing beer throughout the year

questions.explanation: A 正确：教授暗示这条“冷链”为现代食品经济打下了基础。B 过度概括：说彻底消除一切食物腐败太绝对。C 逻辑反转：采冰业让普通家庭更容易储存生鲜，而非更难。D 话题相邻：酿酒只是众多用途之一，说“只对酿酒有用”过窄且不符。

items.subject: ecology
items.topic: how mycorrhizal fungal networks let trees share resources underground
items.transcript: When we look at a forest, we tend to see a collection of individual trees competing for light and water. But beneath the soil, something more cooperative is happening. Most trees are connected to one another through a vast underground web of fungi, and through that web they can actually share resources. Ecologists sometimes call this network the wood wide web. Here's how it works. The roots of most plants form a partnership with fungi called mycorrhizae. The fungus wraps around or grows into the roots and extends thread-like filaments far out into the soil. The tree supplies the fungus with sugars it makes through photosynthesis. In return, the fungus gathers water and nutrients like phosphorus and delivers them to the tree. It's a classic exchange that benefits both partners. But the fungal threads don't just link one tree to one fungus. They connect many trees together into a single network. Researchers have found that carbon can move through this network from one tree to another. In one famous experiment, an ecologist labeled the carbon in certain trees with a traceable isotope. She found that carbon flowed from trees growing in bright sunlight to shaded seedlings that couldn't make enough of their own. Larger, older trees often act as hubs, supporting younger ones nearby. This changes how we think about forests. A forest may function less like a crowd of rivals and more like an interconnected community, where the health of the whole depends on hidden partnerships we are only beginning to understand.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How a single fungus attaches itself to a tree's roots
options.B: How trees compete with one another for sunlight
options.C: How underground fungal networks let trees share resources
options.D: How forests are now mapped using internet technology

questions.explanation: C 正确：全篇讲地下真菌网络如何让树木互相共享资源。A 太狭窄：单株树与真菌的结合只是机制的一部分。B 话题错位：讲座恰恰是要推翻“树只是彼此竞争”的看法。D 表面词陷阱：借“wood wide web / 网”一词误导为互联网测绘，与内容无关。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what does the tree provide to the fungus?

options.A: Phosphorus gathered from deep within the soil
options.B: Shelter for the fungus among its high branches
options.C: Water that it absorbs through its leaves by day
options.D: Sugars that the tree makes through photosynthesis

questions.explanation: D 正确：教授说树把光合作用产生的糖分供给真菌。A 错误细节：磷是真菌提供给树的，方向反了。B 话题相邻：树枝提供庇护听似合理，但课上未提。C 逻辑反转：水和养分是真菌收集后给树的，而非树经叶片吸收给真菌。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did the isotope experiment reveal?

options.A: Carbon flowed from sunlit trees to shaded seedlings
options.B: Seedlings grew faster when cut off from the network
options.C: Fungi competed with trees for scarce soil nutrients
options.D: Carbon moved from shaded seedlings to sunlit trees

questions.explanation: A 正确：实验用同位素标记发现碳从阳光充足的树流向遮荫下的幼苗。B 错误细节：课上未提断网后幼苗长得更快，且与共享主旨相反。C 话题相邻：真菌与树争养分听似合理，但实验讲的是碳的流动而非竞争。D 逻辑反转：碳的流向恰好相反。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about how we should view forests?

options.A: Trees never compete with each other for any resources
options.B: A forest works partly as an interconnected community
options.C: Individual trees survive best in complete isolation
options.D: Fungi matter far more to a forest than the trees do

questions.explanation: B 正确：教授暗示森林在一定程度上像一个相互连接的群落，而非纯粹的竞争者集合。A 过度概括：说树“从不”竞争太绝对，开头也承认存在对光水的竞争。C 逻辑反转：与相互连接、彼此支持的主旨相反。D 话题相邻：真菌很重要，但没有说它比树更重要。

items.subject: art_history
items.topic: how sfumato created soft, hazy transitions in Renaissance painting
items.transcript: Look closely at the Mona Lisa, and you'll notice something unusual about her face. There are no hard lines around the eyes or the corners of the mouth. Instead, the forms seem to melt gently into shadow. This soft, smoky quality has a name: sfumato. The word comes from the Italian fumo, meaning smoke, and it describes a technique that transformed Renaissance painting. Before sfumato, painters often defined shapes with clear, sharp outlines, almost like a drawing filled in with color. Leonardo da Vinci pioneered a different approach. Rather than drawing a boundary and coloring inside it, he built up form using extremely thin, translucent layers of paint called glazes. He would apply glaze after glaze, sometimes dozens of them, letting each one dry. The result was a gradual, almost imperceptible transition from light to dark, with no visible edge where one tone became another. Why did this matter so much? Consider how we actually see the world. In real life, edges are rarely crisp; objects blur slightly into their surroundings, especially in dim light or at a distance. By softening his transitions, Leonardo made his figures look more three-dimensional and lifelike than anything painted before. The haziness also lends his faces a sense of mystery, since an expression that never quite resolves invites the viewer to keep searching it. Sfumato became one of the most admired achievements of the High Renaissance. Later painters studied Leonardo's smoky shadows for generations, and the technique remains a benchmark for depicting the subtle play of light on the human face.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why the Mona Lisa's smile appears so mysterious
options.B: How Renaissance painters mixed their own paint colors
options.C: How smoke was depicted in Italian Renaissance paintings
options.D: A painting technique that softens transitions between light and shadow

questions.explanation: D 正确：讲座主题是晕涂法（sfumato），一种柔化明暗过渡的绘画技法。A 太狭窄：蒙娜丽莎的神秘只是用来引入技法的例子。B 话题错位：调色不是本讲内容。C 表面词陷阱：sfumato 源自“烟”，但讲的是技法效果，而非描绘烟雾。

questions.stem: According to the professor, how did Leonardo build up his forms?

options.A: By layering many thin, translucent glazes of paint
options.B: By outlining shapes and filling them with flat color
options.C: By carving fine details into a layer of wet plaster
options.D: By using a single thick, opaque layer of paint

questions.explanation: A 正确：教授说达·芬奇用许多极薄的透明罩染层层叠加来塑形。B 错误细节：那是 sfumato 之前的旧做法，正是被对比的对象。C 话题相邻：在湿灰泥上刻画属于壁画工艺，与此无关。D 逻辑反转：与“薄而透明的多层”相反。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention how we see the world in real life?

options.A: To explain why Leonardo painted mostly outdoor scenes
options.B: To explain why softened edges look more lifelike
options.C: To point out that edges in real life look blurry
options.D: To argue that realism is the only goal of all painting

questions.explanation: B 正确：提到现实中边缘并不锐利，是为解释为何柔化过渡会显得更真实立体。A 目的错位：讲座并未谈到户外作画。C 字面复述：只是复述“现实边缘模糊”这句话，而非解释其用意。D 过度推断：教授没有主张写实是绘画唯一目标。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the mysterious quality of Leonardo's faces?

options.A: Every painting with soft edges looks equally mysterious
options.B: Sharp, crisp outlines make a face look more mysterious
options.C: The unresolved expressions draw viewers to keep looking
options.D: The mystery comes mainly from the subjects' clothing

questions.explanation: C 正确：教授暗示柔化使表情“始终不完全确定”，从而引人不断端详，产生神秘感。A 过度概括：并非所有柔边画都同样神秘，这把个案放得太大。B 逻辑反转：清晰锐利的轮廓恰恰是柔化的反面。D 话题相邻：把神秘感归于服饰，课上未提，属无关联想。
